title: Propagation and localization of spin excitations at altermagnetic domain walls
authors: Oksana Peschanska, Jeroen van den Brink, Volodymyr P. Kravchuk
abstract: Altermagnets (A$\ell$Ms) are spin-compensated materials in which opposite-spin sublattices are connected by a symmetry that causes a spin splitting in their elementary excitations. As there is a strong effect of altermagnetism on domain wall properties, it is quite natural to also expect an enrichment of the physics of magnetic excitations at A$\ell$M domain walls. Here, we consider the propagation of spin eigen-excitations along domain walls in easy-axial $d$-wave A$\ell$Ms. Investigating the presence of bound states localized on a domain wall, we find that the effect of the A$\ell$M on the bound states strongly depends on the orientation of the domain wall relative to the crystallographic directions. If the domain wall is oriented along a nodal direction [100] or [010], A$\ell$M does not change the number of bound states; however, it leads to a nonlinear dispersion and a tilt of the wavefront. The effect of A$\ell$M is strongest when the domain wall is oriented along the directions [110] or [$\bar{1}$10], i.e., along the directions of the strongest A$\ell$M splitting in the magnon spectrum. In this case, (i) the additional gapped bound states appear, (ii) degeneracy of the eigenstates with respect to their polarization (right-handed or left-handed precession of the N{é}el vector) is removed, and (iii) the localization area of the bound states strongly depends on the eigenfrequency. The latter may lead to strong localization of the bound state at the domain wall. We further consider the influence of a static magnetic field that is applied along the easy axis, and find that the magnetic field induces an asymmetry between the localization regions on opposite sides of the domain wall and sets an upper limit on the absolute value of the propagating eigenstate's wave vector.
summary: This paper theoretically examines spin wave propagation and localization at domain walls in altermagnetic materials. By analyzing the system's Lagrangian, the authors show that the A$\ell$M interaction strongly modifies the excitation spectrum, particularly when the wall aligns along specific crystallographic directions. The findings predict the emergence of new bound states and field-induced asymmetries, offering insights into controlling magnetic excitations.
main_problem: The study investigates how altermagnetism (A$\ell$M) modifies the propagation and localization of spin excitations at magnetic domain walls in easy-axial $d$-wave materials.
main_result: The A$\ell$M effect strongly depends on the domain wall orientation, leading to additional gapped bound states and removing eigenstate polarization degeneracy when the wall is oriented along specific high-symmetry directions like [110].
method: The analysis uses a Lagrangian approach derived from the nonlinear sigma model, linearizing excitations via Holstein-Primakoff representation, and solving resulting differential equations (often mapping to hypergeometric forms).
model_or_system: The system is an altermagnetic material ($	ext{A}\ell	ext{M}$) characterized by spin compensation and symmetry-induced spin splitting. The dynamics are modeled using a Lagrangian incorporating terms for antiferromagnetic stiffness, easy-axial anisotropy, and the A$\ell$M coupling strength ($A_{alt}$).
key_observables: Bound state existence, dispersion relation $\omega(k_y)$, localization area of bound states, and asymmetry in eigenstate intensity distribution.
important_parameters: The dimensionless A$\ell$M parameter ($\epsilon$), the domain wall orientation relative to crystallographic axes, and the strength of an applied static magnetic field ($B$).
main_assumptions: Approximations include using the exchange approximation for spin dynamics and linearizing excitations around a static domain wall profile. The mathematical analysis often relies on mapping to solvable differential equations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate dispersion relations comparing AFM vs A$\ell$M cases, showing localization region size dependence on eigenfrequency, and demonstrating spatial asymmetry induced by an external magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the general Lagrangian description of spin dynamics to analyzing planar wave excitations. It then focuses on solving for bound states using reduced Lagrangians, comparing results across different domain wall orientations (nodal vs maximal splitting) and finally incorporating the effects of a static magnetic field.
why_interesting: This work provides detailed theoretical predictions about how subtle symmetry-breaking mechanisms (like altermagnetism) fundamentally alter collective excitations (magnons) at structural defects (domain walls), which is crucial for spintronics applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Profile of a static Bloch DW (ϕdw = −π/2). Arrows represent N´eel vector orientation, and the density plot shows the distribution of its z-component. The reference frame is chosen such that ex and ey are always normal and tangential to the DW, respectively. Orientation of the DW relative to the crystallographic directions is controlled by the angle φ0.

FIG. 2. (a) – Dispersion relations (3) for φ0 = −π/4 and φ0 = π/4 are shown by solid and dashed lines, respectively. In both cases, kX = 0, and Λ = εΩafmrℓ2 with ε = 0.25. The positive and negative frequencies correspond to the right- handed and left-handed precession of the N´eel vector above its equilibrium orientation. (b) – isosurfaces of constant energies |Ω| = 2Ωafmr.

FIG. 3. Dispersion relations for the localized translational mode for the AFM and AℓM DWs oriented in a nodal di- rection are shown on panels (a) and (c), respectively. The distribution of the mode amplitude and the corresponding de- formation of the DW profile are shown on panels (b) (AFM), and (d) (AℓT) for ky = 1.

FIG. 4. Properties of the bound eigenstates of an AℓT DW oriented in the direction of the maximal AℓT splitting (φ0 = −π/4). Panel (a) shows the dependence of the first four (n = 0, . . . 3) bound-state eigenfrequencies on the value of the AℓT parameter for the case ky = 0; The inset shows the eigenstates’ profiles. (b) Dispersion relation of the bound eigenstates for ε = 0.85; (c) Examples of the spatial distributions of the eigenstates’ amplitude and the corresponding deformation of the DW profile for the points (i) – (iv) shown on panel (b).

FIG. 5. Dependence of the size of the localization region of different bound states on their eigenfrequency for the case φ0 = −π/4, ε = 0.85. The inset shows the frequency- dependence of the group velocity vg = ∂kyω; solid and dashed lines correspond to the cases ky > 0 and ky < 0, respectively.
